s: Inside the workspace.
A finished run opens as a workspace in AgX Studio — Graph, Plan, Report, and Doc views over the run's trace, with an Agent panel beside them.
You steer the run in plain language, add steps, ask about any node, and improve flagged results, until it assembles a publication-style report and document.
Interactive demo — every agent reply below is pre-scripted and every number is an illustrative placeholder; the live Studio agent panel ships with the release.
Follow every decision.
Every run produces a trace: the plan, each agent step, verifier decisions, and the provenance trail.
The release will ship an immutable trace viewer over sanitized public bundles.
Below is a self-contained illustrative demo — no network requests, no real results.
Deconvolution deployment — illustrative demo trace.
How it works.

Turning raw measurements into a trustworthy image takes far more than calling a solver: forward models must be checked, hyperparameters tuned on the right split, metrics computed consistently, and every claim traced back to raw arrays.
AgX runs that whole loop.
Deployment.
Given a new imaging problem — forward model, data, goal — AgX adapts known solvers, tunes them honestly on validation data, and delivers verified reconstructions on held-out test data.
Discovery.
AgX proposes candidate reconstruction-algorithm variants, benchmarks them under matched budgets, and reports which survive skeptical review — including the ones that don't.
Full architecture and the two modes in depth → the technical report.
Honest by construction.
The point of AgX is not a leaderboard — it is evidence you can trust.
The verification and provenance machinery runs inside the loop, so an unverifiable or physics-inconsistent result is blocked, not reported.
Measurement fidelity.
Image-space metrics alone can never approve a result.
The relative residual ‖A(x̂)−y‖ / ‖y‖ must be consistent with the declared noise level, or the reconstruction is flagged as physics-inconsistent — however good its PSNR looks.
Adversarial verification.
A skeptical advisor watches every run and blocks reporting on missing artifacts, hash mismatches, or too-good-to-be-true results (triggering a dedicated attack budget).
Code changes pass a multi-agent review before they execute.
Provenance & replay.
Every reported number carries an immutable record — raw arrays, per-sample metrics, code/config hashes, compute environment, exact command.
Figures cite records by id; replay tooling re-verifies hashes and recomputes metrics from frozen arrays.
Frozen ≠ publishable.
A core rule: an artifact can be sealed, hashed, and reproducible and still not be a public claim until its evidence gate passes and a human approves it.
The verifier stack, provenance schema, and record format in full → the technical report.
Evidence.

Public evidence comes from a preregistered, sealed, label-blind protocol: protocols, budgets, seeds, and stop rules are cryptographically sealed before held-out labels are revealed, and scoring happens once, after an authorized reveal.
Until that completes and a human release gate signs off, this site shows no real evidence numbers.

When the sealed protocol completes, every cell is generated from the frozen claim manifest and links to a traceable evidence ID — including null and inconclusive results, which stay visible rather than being dropped.
The full results table will live in the report.
Report & docs.
Detailed material will live in the technical report and the documentation — this page stays focused on the interface.
Everything below is prepared under the same evidence discipline and links here, with checksums, when the human release gate approves publication.
Technical report pending gates.
System architecture, both modes, the verifier and provenance subsystems, matched-budget agent ablations, and an honest account of failure modes — on arXiv.
Every quantitative claim resolves to an approved evidence record.
Documentation pending.
Versioned and executable: install & doctor, zero-to-first-run (replay a bundled run with no credentials), concepts, deployment guides, and CLI/config reference.
Examples pending.
Runnable, pinned examples — including a negative case where the verifier correctly blocks a bad result, the fastest way to understand what its "yes" means.
Tutorial.
A short screencast will walk through one full AgX session end to end — posing an imaging problem, watching the agents plan, implement, run, and verify it, and reading the provenance-locked result.
Tutorial video pending — recorded and captioned as part of the release, embedded here as a self-hosted file with a transcript; no third-party player or network request.
Until it ships, the workspace and trace demos above show the same end-to-end flow.
Related work.
AgX sits where three fast-moving lines of work meet — and takes a deliberate position within them.
ML for science.

Machine learning is now a primary engine of discovery — protein structure and design (AlphaFold 3[1], crowned by the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry), materials (GNoME[2], MatterGen[3]), weather (GraphCast[4], GenCast[5] — now operational at ECMWF), and mathematics (FunSearch[6], AlphaProof[7]) — increasingly organized around scientific foundation models.
Agentic AI for research.
The frontier is autonomous: “AI-scientist” systems (Sakana AI[8], Google’s AI co-scientist[9]) and agent-run chemistry and materials labs (Coscientist[10], ChemCrow[11], A-Lab[12]).

Yet research-agent benchmarks stay low — MLE-bench[13] ~17%, ScienceAgentBench[14] ~32%, CORE-Bench[15] ~19% on the hardest tasks — and documented failures (reward-hacked evaluations, contested “discoveries,” irreproducible results) are common.
The science of ML — and a rigor crisis.

As ML becomes an object of study (scaling laws, interpretability, the unsettled “emergence” debate[16]), it inherits the failures it imports elsewhere: leakage-driven irreproducibility[17] across hundreds of papers, and a wave of AI-generated “slop” straining peer review.
The common bottleneck is verification: generation has outpaced validation.

AgX’s response is to fold skepticism into the loop — measurement-fidelity checks, enforced validation/test discipline, adversarial multi-agent review, and provenance-locked results — so that connecting scientists to the best of AI/ML also yields results they can trust.
References.
Ethics & social impact.
Why it matters.

Autonomous scientific agents can produce figures, metrics, and even papers far faster than anyone can check them — and the field is already paying for it in reward-hacked benchmarks, irreproducible findings, and AI-generated “slop.
An agent that optimizes a number while ignoring the physics, the data split, or the provenance is not a scientific tool; it is a liability.
Our stance.

AgX is built so that speed does not cost trust: every reported number binds to raw artifacts and a provenance record; measurement-fidelity and validation/test discipline are enforced, not optional; results are cross-examined by adversarial verifiers; and publication stays a human-gated act — the automation prepares the evidence, a person approves it.
Limits and dual use.
AgX accelerates the search over methods; it does not replace domain expertise, experimental validation, or peer review, and its outputs are proposals to be checked, not settled facts.
The same automation that speeds honest work could amplify careless or bad-faith work — which is precisely why we invest in verification, not just generation.
Access.
We want the interface itself to be usable by as many people as possible; see the accessibility statement.
Notes.
AgX is the formula for a silver halide — silver (Ag) plus a halogen (X) — the light-sensitive compound behind photographic film.
Agentic Imaging” abbreviates to AgI, silver iodide; writing the halogen as the variable X signals that the pipeline reaches beyond imaging to research across AI/ML and the sciences.
Every number on this page is an illustrative placeholder until sealed evidence is revealed and human-approved.
This site shows the interface — the numbers will live in the report.
Discovery-mode results are reported under matched compute budgets against tuned classical and learned baselines; proposals that do not beat their baselines are reported as such.
Reconstructions whose residual exceeds a small multiple of the declared noise level are flagged as physics-inconsistent regardless of how good their image-space metrics look.
Publication itself — the arXiv submission, code release, and this site's evidence pages going live — is a human-executed transaction; the automation prepares it but cannot perform it.
